data.title: How many Fed rate cuts in 2026?
Who will Trump nominate as Fed Chair?
What will Fed Rate hit before 2027?
Which countries will Trump make new trade deals with before 2027?
Will Trump create a tariff dividend by March 31?

data.data:
[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-04] Côte d'Ivoire port arrivals reached 1.34 MMT by March 1, down 3.6% year-on-year, with total 2025/26 production projected to fall 10.8% to 1.65 MMT. [2026-03-01] Côte d'Ivoire officially launched its mid-crop season early on March 1 to address an excess stock crisis, with an estimated 200,000 MT of unsold cocoa accumulating due to price standoffs. [2026-02-25] Meteorological projections for southern Ghana indicate the major rainy season began in late February, though localized dry spells are expected in March. [2026-03-03] The ICCO confirmed a global cocoa surplus of 75,000 MT for the 2024/25 season.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis contained a critical hallucination by referencing 'drastic farmgate price cuts in Ghana (-29%)' which was not present in the evidence. It also exhibited confirmation bias by heavily weighting the bearish short-term data while largely dismissing the bullish long-term production deficits. A corrected assessment reveals a market experiencing a severe short-term commercial bottleneck masking a tightening long-term supply. In the immediate term, bearish pressures dominate: the ICCO confirmed a 75,000 MT global surplus for 2024/25, and Côte d'Ivoire is struggling with 200,000 MT of unsold upcountry stock due to price standoffs, forcing an early mid-crop launch to clear inventory. Additionally, early rains in Ghana generally support mid-crop development. However, these bearish physical gluts are counterbalanced by bullish forward-looking indicators, specifically Côte d'Ivoire's 3.6% lag in current port arrivals and a projected 10.8% decline in 2025/26 production. While the structural supply outlook is tightening, the immediate necessity to liquidate massive unsold inventories alongside a confirmed global surplus dictates a bearish near-term sentiment.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-04] EUR/USD fell to 1.1600 (a 3-month low), with MUFG lowering its end-Q1 forecast to 1.1500 due to energy pressures and Middle East conflict safe-haven USD demand. - [2026-03-03] Cocoa futures dropped to $3,015/tonne (-26.21% m/m) driven by a projected 365,000-ton global surplus. - [2026-02-25 to 2026-02-27] Ghana and Ivory Coast cut official farmgate prices by roughly 30-35% to incentivize international sales. - Rising shipping and insurance costs linked to the Middle East conflict are further discouraging buyers.
[ANALYSIS]: The bearish outlook for cocoa is heavily supported by a confluence of fundamental oversupply and macroeconomic headwinds. Fundamentally, a projected 365,000-ton global surplus has already driven futures down 26.21% month-over-month to $3,015/tonne. This glut has forced major producers Ghana and Ivory Coast to cut farmgate prices by 30-35% to stimulate international sales. Macroeconomically, the USD's strength—highlighted by EUR/USD falling to 1.1600 and MUFG forecasting a further drop to 1.1500—makes USD-denominated cocoa more expensive for European buyers, suppressing demand. Furthermore, rising shipping and insurance costs due to Middle East tensions are squeezing buyer margins. While the CFA franc's peg to the weakening Euro technically improves local currency conversion for West African exporters, this minor FX benefit is vastly outweighed by the severe downward price pressure from the global supply glut, weak European purchasing power, and rising freight costs.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1) [2026-03-04] Ivory Coast government committed to purchasing 100,000 MT of residual stock at the guaranteed 2,800 CFA price to prevent threatened strikes and administrative blockades by farmers in Duekoue and San Pedro. 2) [2026-03-02] Ghana's COCOBOD reported a debt of GHS 32.5 billion, alongside a GH₵30 billion working capital shortfall that forced 10-20% executive pay cuts. 3) [2026-03-01] Côte d'Ivoire officially advanced its mid-crop season to March, slashing farmgate prices to 800-1,000 CFA/kg. 4) [Raw Research] These policy shifts have created inverted smuggling incentives and elevated supply-side risks due to the financial instability of state-run boards.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis suffered from confirmation bias by framing inherently bearish physical supply indicators—such as the early launch of the mid-crop and the existence of 100,000 MT of residual stock—exclusively as bullish supply chain failures. A more objective evaluation reveals a market torn between bearish physical availability and bullish institutional risks. On the bearish side, Côte d'Ivoire's ability to advance the mid-crop to March 1 and slash farmgate prices to 800-1,000 CFA/kg indicates immediate physical supply is coming online. However, severe structural risks threaten the delivery of this supply. Ghana's COCOBOD is facing a severe liquidity crisis with a GHS 32.5 billion debt and GH₵30 billion shortfall (March 2), crippling its procurement capacity. In Côte d'Ivoire, the drastic price cuts have sparked strike threats, forcing a March 4 emergency government intervention to buy residual stock at the old 2,800 CFA price to prevent blockades. Consequently, while physical beans exist, financial instability, civil unrest, and inverted smuggling incentives severely threaten commercial export channels, resulting in a highly volatile but fundamentally mixed outlook.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: - Ivory Coast cocoa arrivals: 1.335M MT as of Mar 1, down 3.7% YoY (Reuters/MarketScreener, 2026-03-02). - Weekly deliveries Feb 23-Mar 1: 15k MT Abidjan (up from prior 12k), 13k MT San Pedro (CocoaIntel, 2026-03-02). - Europe Q4 2025 grindings: -8.3% YoY to 304k MT, 12-yr low (ECA, 2026-01-15). - ICCO 2024/25: 75k MT surplus (prod +8.4%, grindings -4.2%) (CocoaIntel, 2026-03-03). - Shipping/insurance costs up for cocoa exports due to Hormuz halt (Barchart/Nasdaq, 2026-03-02), though primary W. Africa-Europe routes less directly impacted.
[ANALYSIS]: Supply resilient (weekly Abidjan deliveries up to 15k MT, cumulative -3.7% YoY mild) amid ICCO-projected 75k MT global surplus (prod growth outpacing grindings decline), while Europe grindings signal weak demand (-8.3% YoY Q4 low). Shipping costs rising per source, adding minor export pressure, but no evidence of physical bottlenecks (e.g., congestion, queues at Abidjan/Tema). Overall excess supply supports bearish price outlook; prior analysis sound but adds nuance on shipping route relevance to avoid over-causation.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1. [2026-03-03] ICE cocoa inventories rose to a 6.5-month high of 2,200,058 bags (Source: Barchart). 2. [2026-03-03] Cocoa prices settled at 3,015 USD/T, representing a 26.21% decline over the past month (Source: Trading Economics). 3. [2026-03-02] ICCO revised end-of-season stocks upward to 1.347 million tonnes, a 5.9% year-on-year increase (Source: CocoaIntel). 4. [2026-02-27] Hedgepoint Global Markets projected a global cocoa surplus of 365,000 tons for the 2025/26 crop year (Source: Trading Economics). 5. [2026-03-02] Ivory Coast cocoa arrivals for 2025/26 reached 1.335 million metric tons, down 3.7% year-over-year (Source: CocoaIntel).
[ANALYSIS]: SELF-CRITIQUE: Three weaknesses are identified in the prior analysis. First, confirmation bias: the prior analysis systematically amplified every bearish data point while dismissing the Ivory Coast arrivals decline (-3.7% YoY) with a single sentence. A genuinely balanced analysis must quantify why 1.335 MMT in arrivals is insufficient to offset the surplus — not merely assert it. If arrivals continue to decline materially into mid-season, the supply overhang could shrink faster than the Hedgepoint/StoneX projections anticipate. Second, false causation risk: the claim that 'international importers are rejecting elevated West African farm-gate prices, causing beans to back up into exchange warehouses' is a plausible mechanism but is not directly evidenced in the cited data packet — it is an inferential narrative, not a confirmed causal chain. This should be labeled as a working hypothesis, not a structural conclusion. Third, confidence inflation: a 0.92 confidence score is difficult to justify when (a) the surplus projection range is wide (287,000–365,000 MT), (b) one key supply indicator (arrivals) is pointing opposite to the bearish thesis, and (c) cocoa prices have already declined 26% in one month, meaning a significant portion of the bearish thesis may already be priced in — reducing expected forward edge. REVISED ASSESSMENT: The weight of evidence remains bearish. ICE certified stocks have risen in a consistent, stepwise trajectory to a 6.5-month high (Barchart, 2026-03-03), the ICCO has confirmed a 5.9% YoY increase in end-of-season stocks to 1.347 MMT (CocoaIntel, 2026-03-02), and independent surplus projections from Hedgepoint (365,000 MT) and StoneX (287,000 MT) converge on a meaningful supply overhang for 2025/26. These are corroborated by hard price action: a 26.21% decline to 3,015 USD/T (Trading Economics, 2026-03-03). However, two risks temper confidence: (1) Ivory Coast arrivals are running 3.7% below prior-year pace, which if sustained could narrow the surplus meaningfully by season-end; and (2) at 3,015 USD/T — down from peaks above 10,000 USD/T in 2024 — markets may have already discounted much of the known bearish information, limiting incremental downside unless stocks continue building at the current pace. The bearish thesis is structurally intact but not risk-free, and confidence is revised downward to MODERATE to reflect these unresolved offsets.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-02-24] COT report: Non-Commercial traders net short -13,280 contracts (38,533 longs vs. 51,813 shorts) (Tradingster). - [2026-03-03] RSI 25.51, oversold territory (Finviz/Barchart). - [2026-03-04] Prices near 2.75-year low amid robust supplies, slack demand; inventories at 6.5-month high of 2,200,058 bags (Nasdaq/Barchart). - [2026-02-24] Open interest dropped >8,000 contracts (-5%) (XTB). - [2026-01-29] StoneX global cocoa surplus forecast 287,000 MT for 2025/26 (StoneX/Nasdaq).
[ANALYSIS]: CRITIQUE: 1. Mild confirmation bias in selecting only bearish indicators; no counter-evidence (e.g., potential demand recovery). Declining OI with net shorts may signal short-covering (bullish), not just bearish sentiment. Oversold RSI implies rebound risk, tempering pure bearishness. No major recency bias or false causation. 2. Sources are specific and cited appropriately. 3. Bearish sentiment justified by fundamentals (surplus, inventories, low prices), but technicals suggest short-term bounce risk. REVISED: Bearish spec positioning and declining OI reflect weak sentiment amid surplus (287k MT), record inventories, and demand slack, driving prices to multi-year lows. Oversold RSI (25.51) signals technical rebound potential via short-covering from crowded shorts, but fundamentals indicate sustained downward pressure unless supply disruptions occur.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-04] Front-month price stabilized around $3,015 USD/T, rebounding from $2,888. [2026-03-03] Short-term moving averages (MA5, MA10, MA20) issued 'Buy' signals, though price remains below the 200-day SMA of 3494.90. [2026-03-03] Front-month RSI is 54 (Neutral) and CCH7 RSI is 36.19. [2026-03-02] Ivory Coast cumulative arrivals are down 3.6% y/y to 1.34 MMT.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis contained a hallucinated metric ('projected 365,000-ton surplus') not present in the evidence and relied on false causation by assuming 'immediate demand is absorbing available supply' without any demand-side data. A more objective reading of the evidence shows a short-term bullish correction within a longer-term bearish trend. The price rebound to $3,015 and subsequent 'Buy' signals from short-term moving averages (MA5, MA10, MA20) indicate positive short-term momentum. This is fundamentally supported by the 3.6% y/y drop in Ivory Coast arrivals, which tightens immediate supply. Furthermore, the neutral front-month RSI (54) and near-oversold CCH7 RSI (36.19) suggest room for continued short-term upside. However, because the price remains firmly below the 200-day SMA (3494.90), the overarching macro trend is still bearish, meaning the previous confidence score of 0.8 was too high for a counter-trend rally.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-04] Cocoa futures prices trading around $3,015 per tonne, recovering slightly from 2.75-year low of $2,888 (Trading Economics). - Implied Volatility for May 2026 (CCK26) at 44.29%, March 2027 (CCH27) at 36.41% to 39.41% (Barchart, 2026-02-27). - Put/Call Premium Ratio of 39.25 for CCH27, indicating heavy downside skew (Barchart). - Option premiums described as 'expensive' with volatility premium (market observations). - Prices down for seven consecutive weeks, 70% below late-2024 peaks (TechRound, 2026-02-26).
[ANALYSIS]: The seven-week price decline to near three-year lows ($3,015/tonne), despite a minor recovery from $2,888, and 70% drop from 2024 peaks align with a projected 2025/26 surplus from oversupply or softening demand. Elevated implied volatility (36-44%) and heavy put/call skew (39.25) signal market anticipation of continued downside risk and demand for protective puts, outweighing supply constraints in West Africa that sustain structural volatility. No strong evidence of imminent reversal.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]
